sanskrit_devanagari:
वेदेषु यज्ञेषु तपःसु चैव दानेषु यत्पुण्यफलं प्रदिष्टम् ।
अत्येति तत्सर्वमिदं विदित्वा योगी परं स्थानमुपैति चाद्यम् ॥ २८ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
vedeṣu yajñeṣu tapaḥsu caiva dāneṣu yat puṇyaphalaṃ pradiṣṭam
atyeti tat sarvam idaṃ viditvā yogī paraṃ sthānam upaiti cādyam || 28 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (canonical body text; vedabase concatenates the two halves; danda restored from gitasupersite)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।8.28।।)
exact IAST (verbatim from page; sandhi conventions match GRETIL)

translations.verbatim_quote: A person who accepts the path of devotional service is not bereft of the results derived from studying the Vedas, performing sacrifices, undergoing austerities, giving charity or pursuing philosophical and fruitive activities. Simply by performing devotional service, he attains all these, and at the end he reaches the supreme eternal abode.

Those who take to this path of devotion get rewarded beyond the fruits of the study of the Vedas, performance of sacrifices, undergoing austerities, giving in charity, etc. Such yogis reach the eternal supreme abode.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: This verse is the summation of the Seventh and Eighth chapters, which particularly deal with Kṛṣṇa consciousness and devotional service. One has to study the Vedas under the guidance of the spiritual master and undergo many austerities and penances while living under his care.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Prabhupada glosses BG 8.28 in the Gaudiya Vaishnava key.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.28 Viditva, having known; idam, this-having fully ascertained and practised what was spoken in the course of determining the answers to the seven questions (put by Arjuna in verse 1 and 2); the yogi atyeti, transcends, goes beyond; tat sarvam, all those; punya-phalam, results of righteou…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads BG 8.28 in the Advaita non-dual key.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.28 Whatever fruit is said to accrue for meritorious actions in the form of the regular study of the Vedas Rg, Yajus, Saman and Atharvan as also for the performance of sacrifices, austerities, gifts — all these does not transcend on knowing this, namely the greatness of the Lord as taught…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads BG 8.28 in the Vishishtadvaita devotional-qualified key.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The subject matter of this chapter which ascertained the meaning of the eight questions posed by Arjuna has beens concluded along with its results. Lord Krishna sums it up with the words tat sarvam punya-phalam meaning all those pious rewards. Whatever merit one receives from study of the…
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Sridhara provides a synthetic gloss bridging Advaita and devotional readings.

disagreements_among_translators.word: atyeti tat sarvam / paraṁ sthānam ādyam

positions.rendering: is not bereft of the results ... attains all these, and at the end reaches the supreme abode
rewarded beyond the fruits of the study of the Vedas ... reach the eternal supreme abode
transcends all those merit-results declared by the scriptures; reaches the supreme primordial State of God, Brahman
by knowing the greatness of the Lord taught in chapters 7-8, regards all merit-fruits as straw; reaches the supreme primal beginningless abode

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The closing verse names a transcendence of all the puṇyaphala-fruits the Vedas promise for study, sacrifice, austerity, charity. Prabhupada and Mukundananda render the verse devotionally — the bhakta is not bereft of merit-fruits but obtains them implicitly via devotion. Shankara reads it sharply: the jñānin transcends these merit-fruits and reaches Brahman directly. Ramanuja reads transcendence as 'regards as straw' in light of the greatness of the Lord. The verse is doctrinally maximally loaded as the chapter-closing summary. The engineering analog (substrate-knowledge transcending the merit-economy of individual deliverables) is a real operational pattern but vastly under-reaches the verse.

literal_meaning: Whatever fruit of merit is declared for the Vedas, sacrifices, austerities, gifts — the yogi transcends all that, knowing this; reaches the supreme primal abode.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The chapter closes: whatever fruit of merit is declared for the Vedas, sacrifices, austerities, gifts — the yogi who knows the chapter's content transcends all of it; reaches the supreme primal abode (paraṁ sthānam ādyam). Shankara: having known what was taught in answer to Arjuna's seven questions, the yogi transcends the puṇyaphala and reaches Brahman directly. Ramanuja: the joy of knowing the Lord's greatness in chapters 7-8 makes all merit-fruits straw; the yogi reaches the supreme primal beginningless abode. The chapter is over.

remaining_gaps: Ramanuja verbatim — page renders commentary in JS; bhagavad-gita.us static-HTML excerpt captured but full Adidevananda edition would deepen the citation.
